coredata.dc:title: Advantages of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a green sensitizer with ZnO nanorod based photoanodes for solar energy conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Majumder, Tanmoy
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal process. Optical absorption study revealed strong ultraviolet photoabsorption for both GQDs. An additional broad absorption band in the visible region has been observed for N doped GQDs. GQDs and N-GQDs both are highly luminescent and exhibit excitation dependent emission behaviors. Photoelectrochemical properties of GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods have been investigated in details. GQD modified nanorod photoanodes demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) than pristine ZnO nanorods. N-GQD sensitized nanorods exhibited better photoelectrochemical properties than pristine GQD due to higher visible photoabsorption and lower interfacial resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Photoelectrochemical cell
ZnO nanorods
Photoconversion efficiency
Photoresponse

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic force microscopy image of (a) GQDs and (b) N doped GQDs. Height distribution histograms of GQDs and N-GQDs are shown at the inset. (c) Typical TEM micrograph of hydrothermally grown N-GQDs. Size distribution histogram is plotted at the inset. (d) High resolution TEM image of N-GQDs.
Full scan XPS spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs. High resolution N1s spectrum of N-GQD is shown at inset.

UV–Visible absorption spectrum and excitation dependent PL spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) N-GQDs. PL excitation wavelengths were varied from 340–420nm. Photographs of GQD and N-GQD solutions under sunlight and UV light of wavelength 360nm (inset).

(a) Typical SEM micrograph of N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods (b) TEM micrograph of N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods after detachment from FTO substrate. High resolution TEM image of N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods (inset). N-GQDs attachments are indicated by arrows.

(a) Linear sweep voltammograms of pristine ZnO NR, GQD-ZnO NR and N-GQD–ZnO NR photoanodes recorded under white light illumination (170mW/cm2). (b) Photoresponse characteristics of pristine ZnO NR, GQD–ZnO NR and N-GQD–ZnO NR photoanodes. (c) Photoconversion efficiency of pristine ZnO NR, GQD–ZnO NR and N-GQD–ZnO NR photoanodes. (d) IPCE (%) vs. wavelength plot of pristine ZnO NR, GQD–ZnO NR and N-GQD–ZnO NR photoanodes.
Electrochemical impedance spectra of pristine ZnO NR, GQD–ZnO NR and N-GQD–ZnO NR photoanodes under white light incident. The equivalent circuit diagram of the device is shown at the inset.

head.ce:title: Advantages of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a green sensitizer with ZnO nanorod based photoanodes for solar energy conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal process. Optical absorption study revealed strong ultraviolet photoabsorption for both GQDs. An additional broad absorption band in the visible region has been observed for N doped GQDs. GQDs and N-GQDs both are highly luminescent and exhibit excitation dependent emission behaviors. Photoelectrochemical properties of GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods have been investigated in details. GQD modified nanorod photoanodes demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) than pristine ZnO nanorods. N-GQD sensitized nanorods exhibited better photoelectrochemical properties than pristine GQD due to higher visible photoabsorption and lower interfacial resistance.

ce:sections.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been attracted much attention due to their interesting electrical and optical properties [1–4]. These nanomaterials have potential applications in several optoelectronic devices such as light emitting diodes [2], field effect transistors [3], photodetectors [4], solar cells [5–8], photocatalysis [9], and photoelectrochemical cells [10–12]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been studied extensively due to their size tunable optical properties similar to semiconductor quantum dots [13]. These zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials are non toxic, environmental friendly, and can be synthesized by low cost chemical process [14]. Doping is a common process to tailor the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots [15]. Several kinds of doping elements like S, N, and B, Si were investigated to modify the properties of GQDs [9,16–18]. Sensitization of GQDs with nanostructured photoanodes is attractive due to their non-toxic nature and strong photoabsorption in UV–visible region depending on their size and doping elements. Sim et al. [10] synthesized N-doped graphene quantum sheets (GQS) as a catalyst with Si nanowire photoanodes for solar-driven hydrogen evolution. N-doped GQS sensitized nanowires showed excellent catalytic activity with applied bias photon to current conversion efficiency 2.29%. Zhang et al. [11] studied N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) sensitized TiO2 inverse opals for photoelectrochemical applications. The observed photocurrent density was 2 times than CQD sensitized TiO2 and 18 times higher than pristine TiO2 photoanode. Guo et al. [12] have attached GQDs on ZnO nanorods using aminopropyl-triethoxysilane linker molecules and investigated photoelectrochemical properties. The sensitized photoanodes showed open circuit voltage (VOC)~0.15V and short circuit current (JSC) ~0.064mA/cm2. The observed VOC and JSC were 1.8 times and 8 times higher than pristine ZnO, respectively.

In this report, we have grown GQDs and N-doped GQDs using a chemical process. The microstructural, optical absorption and emission properties have been investigated for both quantum dots. The role of GQD and N-GQD as a green sensitizer with ZnO nanorod photoanodes has been explored in details.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal process [19]. The detailed experimental procedure and characterization techniques were presented in supplementary materials. The surface topography of GQDs and N-GQDs was studied using atomic force microscopy (AFM) in tapping mode. Fig. 1 (a) and (b) represents plane view AFM micrographs of GQDs and N-GQDs, deposited on p-Si substrates, respectively. The height distributions of GQDs have been plotted at the inset of Fig. 1(a) and (b). The heights of pristine GQDs and N-doped GQDs are mostly distributed in the range of 0.5–3.5nm and 0.5–2nm, respectively. AFM study clearly indicates that, N-GQDs consist of 1–5 monolayers of graphene, whereas GQDs are composed of 1–8 graphene layers [20]. Fig. 1(c) shows the typical TEM micrograph of N-GQDs of diameter between 3 to 7nm. The size distribution is plotted at the inset of Fig. 1(c). The high resolution TEM micrograph of N-GQDs is presented in Fig. 1(d). N-GQDs are highly crystalline with interplanar spacing of 0.34nm, which is similar to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Inset of Fig. 1d) [21].

The full range XPS spectra of GQD and N-GQDs are presented in Fig. 2 . The dominant binding energy peaks at 283.2eV and 531.2eV, are attributed to the presence of C 1s and O 1s electrons, which are the key elements of GQDs. However, N-GQDs showed an extra peak at 399.2eV, which is ascribed to N1s electrons. The nitrogen atom configurations in GQDs were analyzed by the deconvolution of N 1s peak into three Gaussian parts (inset of Fig. 2). The deconvoluted peaks are located at 398.3eV, 399.6eV and 401.5eV and attributed to Pyridinc, Pyrrolic and Graphitic nitrogen configurations, respectively [22,23]. The optical absorption and emission behaviors of GQDs and N-GQDs were studied after dispersion in aqueous solutions. Fig. 3(a) and (b) represents UV–VIS absorption and emission spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs. The absorption spectrum of GQDs shows two major peaks of wavelength 215nm and 338nm. On the other hand, N-GQDs show two dominant peaks located at 238nm and 334nm, in addition to a broad absorption band in the range 460 to 500nm. The origin of such UV absorption peaks at 215–238nm is attributed to π → π ⁎ transition of CC bonds [9,19,24,25] and at 334–338nm corresponds to n → π ⁎ transition of CO bonds [9]. The broad absorption band of N-GQDs observed at 460–500nm is due to n → π ⁎ transition of CN bonds [25]. The room temperature photoluminescence spectra (PL) of GQDs and N-GQDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths varied from 340–420nm (Fig. 3 (a) and (b)). The PL study clearly indicates that, both GQDs and N-GQDs are highly luminescent. Under sunlight, the GQDs solution is colorless and N-GQDs solution is brown. However, after illumination with UV light (360nm), both emits strong blue wavelength ( ̴ 437nm).The photographs of GQD and N-GQD solutions under sunlight and UV light are presented at inset of Fig. 3(a) and (b), respectively. Excitation wavelength dependent emission behaviors of GQD and N-GQD samples have been observed in our present study. As the excitation wavelength increases from 340–420nm, the PL peak positions have been red shifted from 436 to 486nm, and 432 to 463nm for GQDs and N-GQDs, respectively. Such emissive behaviors are often observed due to the various size distributions and presence of impurity states in graphene quantum dots [23,26].

To elucidate the role of graphene quantum dots as a photosensitizer, photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties of pristine ZnO, GQD and N-GQD decorated ZnO nanorods were studied. ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) were grown on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates using hydrothermal thermal process [27]. The detailed experimental procedures have been reported in supporting results. Fig. 4(a) shows the typical SEM micrograph of N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorod photoanodes. The micrograph revealed the growth of single crystalline hexagonal ZnO nanorods of diameter ranges from 100nm to 250nm. The surface morphology of nanorods, remains unaltered even after attachment of N-GQDs. However, the nanorod surfaces are more uneven than pristine ZnO, which is attributed to the presence of N-GQDs. Fig. 4(b) shows the plane view TEM micrograph of N-GQD decorated ZnO nanorods after detachment from FTO substrates. The estimated average length and diameter of ZnO nanorods are 1.3μm and 200nm, respectively. ZnO nanorods are single crystalline wurtzite structure and the growth direction is along (002) plane with lattice spacing 0.26nm (Supporting Fig. S1). The magnified TEM micrograph (Inset of Fig. 4(b)) shows several small circular spots (indicated by arrows) that correspond to N-doped GQDs, attached on nanorods surface. The energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) study showed the presence of elements Zn, O, N, and C. These elements confirmed the attachment of N-GQDs on ZnO NRs surfaces (Supporting Fig. S2). Fig. 5(a) represents the linear sweep voltammograms (J–V) of pristine ZnO nanorods, GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods under illumination of broad band light of intensity 170mW/cm2. The J–V characteristics of GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO photoanodes were measured at different attachment time (Supporting Fig. S3 and S4). The optimum result for each photoanode at illumination has been presented in Fig. 5(a). Under dark condition, the current density value for all samples including pristine ZnO nanorods is very small and in the range 2–10μA/cm2 at 1V vs Ag/AgCl. Interestingly, upon illumination of light, the current density enhances to 1.23mA/cm2 at 1V vs Ag/AgCl for pristine ZnO nanorods. More importantly, GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods showed significant enhancement of photocurrent. At identical conditions, the photocurrent density for GQD and N-GQD sensitized nanorods is 2.2mA/cm2 and 2.5mA/cm2, respectively. Fig. 5(b) shows the photoresponse curve (J–t) of pristine ZnO, GQD-ZnO and N-GQD-ZnO NRs at 0V vs. Ag/AgCl. Under illumination, the observed photocurrent density (J
SC ) for ZnO, GQD-ZnO and N-GQD-ZnO photoanodes is 0.35mA/cm2, 0.56mA/cm2 and 0.81mA/cm2, respectively. The short circuit current (J
SC ) for GQD-ZnO and N-GQD-ZnO nanorods is 1.4 times and 2.3 times higher than pristine ZnO, respectively. Moreover, the higher J
SC value for N-GQD-ZnO nanorods, indicates the superior photoabsorption and better carrier transport due to N doping effect in GQDs.

The photoconversion efficiency profiles (i.e. photon to hydrogen) of pristine ZnO NR, GQD-ZnO NR and N-GQD-ZnO NR samples have been plotted in Fig. 5(c). The maximum efficiency is obtained 0.83% (at 0.34V vs. Ag/AgCl) for N-GQD-ZnO NR photoanodes. On the other hand, GQD-ZnO nanorods show the efficiency 0.71% at 0.44V vs. Ag/AgCl and pristine ZnO nanorods show the efficiency 0.32% at 0.32V vs. Ag/AgCl. It is obvious that, N-GQD sensitized nanorod photoanodes demonstrate better photocurrent and photoconversion efficiency. To investigate, the role of N doped GQDs for superior photoconversion, incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements have been carried out at 0V vs Ag/AgCl. Fig. 5(d) shows IPCE (%) spectra of pristine ZnO, GQD-ZnO and N-GQD-ZnO photoanodes. The maximum IPCE (%) values for pristine ZnO NRs, GQD-ZnO NRs and N-GQD-ZnO NRs, are obtained as 26.4%, 36.9% and 60.6%, respectively. The higher IPCE (%) of N-GQD-ZnO NR photoanode is attributed to the better photoabsorption, larger carrier lifetime and efficient electron–hole conduction. To analyze the interface properties of GQD/ZnO and N-GQD/ZnO nanorod junctions, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in the frequency interval 1Hz to 1MHz under dark (Supporting Fig. S5) and white light illumination. The plot of real part Re (Z) and imaginary part Im (Z) of complex impedance [Z(ω)= Re(Z)+ jIm(Z)] of all photoanodes under illumination condition are presented in Fig. 6 . Equivalent circuit diagram of ZnO nanorod/electrolyte interface is shown at the inset of Fig. 6. The symbols RS, CPES and RCTs are the series resistance, capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance, respectively. CPEd and RCTd are the capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance at semiconductor-electrolyte junction. Under illumination, the resistance RCTd decreases rapidly for all photoanodes. As for example, at dark condition RCTd(dark) values for ZnO NRs, GQD-ZnO NRs and N-GQD-ZnO NRs are 40, 40 and 38.3kΩcm−2, respectively (Supporting T1). Interestingly, upon illumination of light, RCTd(light) values for the above samples are 287, 168 and 147Ωcm−2, respectively. The rapid decrease of RCTd in GQD modified nanorods is ascribed to faster charge transfer and effective separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs at nanorod/electrolyte junction [28]. More importantly, nitrogen doping effect significantly improves the interfacial resistances between N-GQD/ZnO junction compared to undoped GQD/ZnO junction. The better photoconversion efficiency, IPCE value and less interfacial resistance (RCTd) suggest that, N doped GQD improves the photoelectrochemical properties of N-GQD sensitized ZnO photoanodes.

In summary, GQDs and N-doped GQDs have been grown by a low cost hydrothermal process. XPS study confirmed three different nitrogen configurations in GQD such as Pyridine-like N, Pyrrole-like N and Graphite-like N. UV–visible absorption study revealed two major absorption peaks for both quantum dots situated at 215–237nm and 337–334nm, which are due to π→π⁎ transition of CC bond and n→π⁎ transition of the CO, respectively. The additional broad peak of N-GQDs, observed in the range of 460–500nm is attributed to n→π⁎ transition of CN bonds. GQD and N-GQDs both are highly luminescent and exhibited excitation dependent emission behaviors. GQD and N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods demonstrated higher photoresponse, photoconversion efficiency and IPCE (%) from pristine nanorods. The superior photoelectrochemical behaviors for sensitized nanorods are attributed to better photoabsorption and lower interfacial resistance between GQD/ZnO interfaces. Overall, N-doping effect in GQD creates less interfacial resistance and favorable band alignment, which significantly improves the photoelectrochemical properties. The present study demonstrates the potential use of doped GQDs as a green sensitizer for ZnO nanorod based photoanodes for electrochemical solar energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was funded by CSIR Extramural Research Scheme, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledge the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for UV–VIS-NIR and AFM characterizations. We also acknowledge MSDC laboratory, Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, NIT, Agartala for electrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.03.018.
